Songtype: alarm call, call, contact call between juvenile and parents
Remarks: Contact call between juvenile and parents prior to roosting. It looks like the parents also sound off an alarm call as they fly leaving the juvenile behind. Juvenile is on a fern frond, after it fell from the home tree, maybe as a result of a heavy downpour. Weather condition:  a strong typhoon is set to strike the area. (Juvenile already has flight feathers, although flight is weak )
This was recorded on a windy day, sky is overcast with a very slight drizzle.

There are two birds on the track. The first one is a yellow-vented bulbul. Or so I think... I am not a specialist, just a happy amateur. The bird I'd like to know the name of starts to sing after a second or two. I am not sure this bird is from the Philippines because the sound only (and always) comes from the same direction, that is the house next door inhabited by a Chinese family. I think they might have a "Chinese" bird in a cage  and the song is that of this bird. The main "chorus" is the one recorded here. Sometimes the bird seems to get lost with the notes and sings something different but somehow close to the main phrase. Then it sings the main tune again. It is very active around 5h-6h am. Then it can be heard throughout the day but only once in a while.
Thanks for your help. If I happen to talk to somebody from the Chinese family, I'll ask them :)

Location: Banica Tributary, Negros Oriental, Central Visayas

Songtype: call, probably a courtship display call
Remarks: A group of birds are calling around the flat top of an electric post with overhanging branches about 20 feet from the ground. Birds from the top of the post are giving of the chattering sound, with two birds that seem to be duelling together, wings flapping and falling off from the top but would free themselves from one another midway down the pole. This occurs from time to time and the chattering sound would get stronger as other birds join in. The distinctive single note call are given off by single birds about 5 feet from the group, which do not seem to join the foray.
